standard_description: Use common, proper, and possessive nouns.
subject: Language

khan_exercises.title: Identifying nouns
khan_exercises.description: Learn to identify nouns in context.

items.question_content:
**Which words in the sentence are nouns?**

>Howard eats a sandwich.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
A noun is a person, place, thing, or idea! 

**Remember:** nouns include proper names.

**"Howard"** is the name of a person, so it's a noun.

A **"sandwich"** is a thing, so it's a noun too.

khan_exercises.title: Singular and plural nouns
khan_exercises.description: Distinguish singular and plural nouns from one another!

items.question_content:
**Which of the nouns in this sentence are plural?**

>Arif enchanted the Queen’s blade with many powerful spells.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
A noun is **singular** when it refers to one thing. 

A noun is **plural** when it refers to *more* than one thing. Plural nouns *usually* end in 's'.
**"Spells"** is a plural noun. Notice how the sentence says there are "many" spells.

items.question_content:
**Which of the nouns in this sentence are plural?**

>Reuben put on goggles to protect his eyes.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
A noun is **singular** when it refers to one thing. 

A noun is **plural** when it refers to *more* than one thing. Plural nouns *usually* end in 's'.
**"Goggles"** and **"eyes"** are both plural nouns.

hints:

**"Squirrels"** is a plural noun.

khan_exercises.title: Common and proper nouns
khan_exercises.description: Learn to distinguish common and proper nouns from one another!

items.question_content:
**Which of the nouns in this sentence are proper nouns?**

>The penguins had always wanted to go to Malaysia.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Proper nouns are the names given to specific people, places, or things.  They're always capitalized.
**"Malaysia"** is the name of a country, so it's a proper noun.

items.question_content:
**Which of the nouns in this sentence are proper nouns?**

>Billy cautiously put the eggs in his basket.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Proper nouns are the names of specific people, places, and things.  They're always capitalized.
**"Billy"** is the name of a person, so it's a proper noun.

khan_exercises.title: Concrete and abstract nouns
khan_exercises.description: Learn to distinguish between abstract and concrete nouns!

items.question_content:
**Which of the nouns in this sentence is a concrete noun?**

>My dreams often feature squirrels.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Stuff that you could physically **touch** (like a mountain, a person, or the moon) is **concrete.**

Stuff that you **couldn’t touch** (like freedom, bravery, or friendship) is **abstract.**

So, for each noun, ask yourself, "Could I touch it?"
You could touch a squirrel, so **"squirrels"** is a concrete noun.

items.question_content:
**Which of the nouns in this sentence is a concrete noun?**


>Fiona took careful aim with her slingshot.

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
Stuff that you could physically **touch** (like a mountain, a person, or the moon) is **concrete.**

Stuff that you **couldn’t touch** (like freedom, bravery, or friendship) is **abstract.**

So, for each noun, ask yourself, "Could I touch it?"

You can touch a person, so a name like **"Fiona"** is a concrete noun.

You can touch a **"slingshot"**, so it's a concrete noun too.

items.question_content:
**Which of the nouns in this sentence is a concrete noun?**


>Harrison had a sudden realization.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Stuff that you could physically **touch** (like a mountain, a person, or the moon) is **concrete.**

Stuff that you **couldn’t touch** (like freedom, bravery, or friendship) is **abstract.**

So, for each noun, ask yourself, "Could I touch it?"
You can touch a person, so a name like **"Harrison"** is a  concrete noun.

items.question_content:
**Which of the nouns in this sentence is a concrete noun?**

>Kyle found his inspiration in the desert.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Stuff that you could physically **touch** (like a mountain, a person, or the moon) is **concrete.**

Stuff that you **couldn’t touch** (like freedom, bravery, or friendship) is **abstract.**

So, for each noun, ask yourself, "Could I touch it?"

You can touch a person, so a name like **"Kyle"** is a concrete noun.

A **"desert"** is a physical place you can be and touch, so it's a concrete noun.

items.question_content:
**Which of the nouns in this sentence is a concrete noun?**

>The bears were cranky after their hibernation.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Stuff that you could physically **touch** (like a mountain, a person, or the moon) is **concrete.**

Stuff that you **couldn’t touch** (like freedom, bravery, or friendship) is **abstract.**

So, for each noun, ask yourself, "Could I touch it?"
A bear is something you could touch (though doing so might not be wise), so **"bears"** is a concrete noun.

So, for each noun, ask yourself, "Could I touch it?"

You can touch a person, so a name like **"Natalie"** is a concrete noun.

A **"forest"** is a physical place you can be and touch, so it's a concrete noun.

>Hannah couldn’t understand the anger her brother showed.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Stuff that you could physically **touch** (like a mountain, a person, or the moon) is **concrete.**

Stuff that you **couldn’t touch** (like freedom, bravery, or friendship) is **abstract.**

So, for each noun, ask yourself, "Could I touch it?"

You can touch a person, so a name like **"Hannah"** is a concrete noun.

You can touch a person, so **"brother"** is a concrete noun.
